title: L02.3.assign stmts
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making
Lecture 2.3: Assignment statements

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin
UNSW




                                                    1
 Once we have created an object in Python, we need a way to identify, refer to, and
                                     use it.




                                                                                     3
 Python: Variables and assignment statements
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 When Python creates an object:

      It stores the object somewhere in the computer’s memory.
      The location of the object is called its memory address.
 Python can find objects using their memory addresses, but:

      Programmers do not know these addresses in advance.
      Addresses can change each time we run the program.

                          It is hard to use memory addresses directly in code. . .




                                                                                     5
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 A variable is a name in our code that Python links to an object’s memory address while it executes a
 program.
                                               name     obj

      We use variables to store and access information.
      This information can be an int, str, or any Python object.
      The object can be used or changed by our code.




                                                                                                        6
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement

 Assignment statements have the form:
                                    variable_name = expression

 On execution, Python:
   1. Checks that variable_name is a valid name
   2. Evaluates the expression
   3. Assigns the instance produced by the expression to variable_name.




                                                                           7
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 The use of the equals sign in the assignment statement may be confusing to new programmers.
                                     variable_name = expression


      Read the statement as variable_name is assigned to the result of the expression.
      Do not interpret this as variable_name is equal to the expression.


                             We will return to this point later in the lecture.




                                                                                               8
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




    Basic assignment statement using the primitive types and None are shown below:

1       x = 5                # int
2       pi_approx = 3.14     # float
3       name = "Alice"       # str
4       is_valid = True      # bool
5       result = None        # NoneType




                                                                                     9
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 Let’s pause and summarise what we’ve learned:

      Identifiers and names:
          Names are imperfect ways to refer to instances.
          The identifier concept ensures that every instance of every class has a unique identity.
          Names are then a way to refer to an identifier.
             • The name of an instance may change – its identifier will not.
             • A name may change the instance to which it relates.
          We can write the link as name          obj

     Variables and assignment statements:
          A variable is a name in our code that Python will link with an instance’s unique identifier as it
          executes a program.
          We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement: variable_name =
          expression




                                                                                                              10
 Python: Expressions
 Python: Expressions




 An expression is a combination of instances, operations, and functions. Python evaluates expressions,
 producing an instance as the result.

 Some expressions using integers are:
      int(1)

      int(1) + int(2)

      abs(int(-2))

      int(9) ** abs(int(-2))



    The examples in this section use int(), float(), and str() constructors instead of literals to draw
  attention to the fact that Python must create an instance of any object before it can perform any work.




                                                                                                            11
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating int(1), Python will:
      Create an int instance with a value of 1


                      An int instance with a value of 1 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                            12
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating int(1) + int(2), Python will:
      Create an int instance with a value of 1
      Create an int instance with a value of 2
      Verify that the int class supports addition
      Create an int instance with a value of 1 plus 2


                      An int instance with a value of 3 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                            13
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating abs(int(-2)), Python will:
      Create an int instance with a value of -2
      Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function
      Create an int instance with a value of the absolute value of -2


                      An int instance with a value of 2 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                            14
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating int(9) ** abs(int(-2)), Python will:
      Create an int instance with a value of 9
      Evaluate the abs(int(-2)) sub-expression
           Create an int instance with a value of -2
           Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function
           Create an int instance with a value of 2
      Verify that the int class supports exponentiation using the ** operator.
      Create an int instance with the value of 9 raised to the 2nd power.


                     An int instance with a value of 81 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                            15
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating float(9) ** abs(int(-2)), Python will:
      Create a float instance with a value of 9
      Evaluate the abs(int(-2)) sub-expression
           Create an int instance with a value of -2
           Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function
           Create an int instance with a value of 2
      Verify that the float class supports exponentiation to an int power using the ** operator.
      Create a float instance with the value of 9 raised to the 2nd power.


                    A float instance with a value of 81.0 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                                   16
 Python: Expressions




 A note about mathematical operators for integers and floating-point instances


 Most algebraic operators work as expected:
      Addition: + (e.g., x + y)
      Subtraction: - (e.g., x - y)
      Multiplication * (e.g., x * y)
      Division: / (e.g., x / y)

 One common algebraic operator works di!erently than expected:
      Exponentiation: **


 Python uses two stars (**) for exponentiation. The caret operator (ˆ) has an entirely di!erent meaning.




                                                                                                           17
 Python: Expressions




    Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

1       "Hello" .upper()


    When evaluating "Hello".upper(), Python will:
         Create a str instance with a “Hello”
         Call the instance method .upper on this instance
         Receive a new instance from the method with all letters in uppercase


                     A str instance with a value of “HELLO” is the result of this expression.




                                                                                                18
 Python: Expressions




    Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

1       (True or False) and False


    When evaluating (True or False) and False, Python will:
         Process the parenthetical phrase
              Create a bool instance with the value of True
              Create a bool instance with the value of False
              Apply the or operator on the previous two instances, yielding True
         Finalise the expression
              Use True as the result of the parenthetical phrase
              Recall the bool instance with the value of False
              Apply the and operator on the previous two instances, yielding False


                          A bool instance with a value of False result of this expression.




                                                                                             19
 Key takeaways




    Identifiers and names:
        Names are imperfect ways to refer to instances.
        The identifier concept ensures that every instance of every class has a unique identity.
        Names are then a way to refer to an identifier.
           • The name of an instance may change – its identifier will not.
           • A name may change the instance to which it relates.
        We can write the link as name          obj

    Variables and assignment statements:
        A variable is a name in our code that Python will link with an instance’s unique identifier as it
        executes a program.
        We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement: variable_name =
        expression

    Expressions:
        An expression is a combination of instances, operations, and functions.
        Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances, producing an instance as the
        result.




                                                                                                            20
